---
title: "Ph.D. Stipends by research topic and universities"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
    self-contained: true
jupyter: python3
---

## Abstract

This project cleans and analyses a dataset of Ph.D. student stipends by university
and department over time.

Key findings:

- The majority of respondents are from the USA; non-US data collection started in 2013.
- Overall pay and living wage ratios are significantly higher in US universities.
- Stipends do not increase with seniority (program year).
- Mean stipends dipped around the 2008 financial crisis.
- Pay varies noticeably across departments.

**Data source:** [Kaggle – PhD Stipends v7](https://www.kaggle.com/paultimothymooney/phd-stipends/data),
downloaded 2020-07-12.

## Setup

```{python}
import os
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mticker
import seaborn as sns

DATA_DIR = "Data"
OUTPUT_DIR = "Output"
os.makedirs(OUTPUT_DIR, exist_ok=True)

COLOR_US = "#CC6666"
COLOR_NON_US = "#9999CC"
```

## Data cleaning

### Load & rename

```{python}
df = pd.read_csv(f"{DATA_DIR}/phd-stipends.csv")

df.rename(columns={
    "Overall Pay":   "Overall_Pay",
    "LW Ratio":      "LW_Ratio",
    "Academic Year": "Academic_Year",
    "Program Year":  "Program_Year",
    "12 M Gross Pay":"Gross_Pay_12M",
    "9 M Gross Pay": "Gross_Pay_9M",
    "3 M Gross Pay": "Gross_Pay_3M",
}, inplace=True)

print(f"{df.shape[1]} variables, {df.shape[0]:,} observations")
```

### Drop high-missingness columns

`Fees`, `Comments`, `Gross_Pay_9M`, and `Gross_Pay_3M` contain a majority of
missing values and are excluded.

```{python}
df.drop(columns=["Fees", "Gross_Pay_9M", "Comments", "Gross_Pay_3M"],
        errors="ignore", inplace=True)
```

### Convert salary columns to numeric

Values are stored as strings (e.g. `"$41,900"`); punctuation is stripped before
casting to float.

```{python}
for col in ("Overall_Pay", "Gross_Pay_12M"):
    if col in df.columns:
        df[col] = pd.to_numeric(
            df[col].astype(str).str.replace(r"[^\d]", "", regex=True),
            errors="coerce",
        )
```

### Normalise Program Year

```{python}
program_year_map = {
    "1st": "1", "2nd": "2", "3rd": "3",
    "4th": "4", "5th": "5", "6th and up": "6",
}
df["Program_Year"] = df["Program_Year"].replace(program_year_map)
df["Seniority_Year"] = pd.to_numeric(df["Program_Year"], errors="coerce")
```

### Parse Academic Year

```{python}
df["Academic_Year"] = (
    df["Academic_Year"].astype(str)
    .str.replace(r"-\d{4}$", "", regex=True)
)
df["Academic_Year"] = pd.to_datetime(df["Academic_Year"], format="%Y", errors="coerce")
```

### Join university locations

US / non-US labels were manually compiled in `Data/univ_location.xlsx`.

```{python}
univ_locations = pd.read_excel(f"{DATA_DIR}/univ_location.xlsx", sheet_name=2)
df = df.merge(univ_locations, on="University", how="inner")
```

### Normalise department names

Several identical departments appear under different spellings. Regex patterns
group them into canonical names; only departments with ≥ 50 observations are
kept for the department-level plots.

```{python}
dept_patterns = [
    (r".*bio.*|.*cell.*|.*molecular.*|.*neuro.*|.*genetics.*", "biology"),
    (r".*aero.*|.*space.*",                                    "aerospace"),
    (r".*health.*|.*medical.*|.*clinical.*|.*pharmacy.*",      "health"),
    (r".*chemical.*|.*chemistry.*",                            "chemistry"),
    (r".*comput.*|^computational.*",                           "computer science"),
    (r".*psych.*",                                             "psychology"),
    (r".*communication.*",                                     "communication"),
    (r".*physics.*",                                           "physics"),
    (r".*agri.*|.*crop.*|.*plant.*",                           "agriculture"),
    (r".*engineering.*|.*mechanical.*",                        "engineering"),
    (r".*business.*",                                          "business"),
    (r".*math.*",                                              "mathematics"),
    (r".*crim.*",                                              "criminology"),
    (r".*earth.*|.*geo.*",                                     "geoscience"),
    (r".*english.*",                                           "english"),
]

df["department_cleaned"] = df["Department"].str.lower()
df = df[df["department_cleaned"].notna() & (df["department_cleaned"] != "n/a")]

for pattern, replacement in dept_patterns:
    df["department_cleaned"] = df["department_cleaned"].str.replace(
        pattern, replacement, regex=True
    )

top_dept = (
    df.groupby("department_cleaned")
    .size()
    .reset_index(name="n")
    .query("n >= 50")["department_cleaned"]
    .tolist()
)
```

### Filter observations

Excluded: missing `Overall_Pay`, salaries above \$50 K (likely entry errors),
missing `LW_Ratio`, missing `Program_Year`.

```{python}
df = df[
    df["Overall_Pay"].notna()
    & (df["Overall_Pay"] <= 50000)
    & df["LW_Ratio"].notna()
    & df["Program_Year"].notna()
]

print(f"{len(df):,} observations retained")
```

## Data visualisation

### Overall Pay & Living Wage Ratio by location

US universities account for the majority of respondents. Both overall pay and
living wage ratios are significantly higher in the USA.

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Overall Pay and Living Wage Ratio by location (pay > $15 K)"

df_high = df[df["Overall_Pay"] > 15000]
palette = {"us": COLOR_US, "non_us": COLOR_NON_US}

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5))

sns.boxplot(x="location", y="Overall_Pay", hue="location",
            data=df_high, palette=palette, legend=False, ax=axes[0])
axes[0].set_title("Overall Pay by location")
axes[0].yaxis.set_major_formatter(
    mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f"${x:,.0f}")
)

sns.boxplot(x="location", y="LW_Ratio", hue="location",
            data=df_high, palette=palette, legend=False, ax=axes[1])
axes[1].set_title("Living Wage Ratio by location")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### Students earning below / above \$15 K

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Count of students below and above $15 K by location"

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 5))

for ax, threshold, label in [
    (axes[0], "below", "Students under $15K"),
    (axes[1], "above", "Students over $15K"),
]:
    subset = (
        df[df["Overall_Pay"] <= 15000]
        if threshold == "below"
        else df[df["Overall_Pay"] > 15000]
    )
    counts = subset["location"].value_counts().reindex(
        ["us", "non_us"], fill_value=0
    )
    bars = ax.bar(counts.index, counts.values,
                  color=[COLOR_US, COLOR_NON_US], width=0.5)
    for bar, val in zip(bars, counts.values):
        ax.text(
            bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2,
            bar.get_height() + 5,
            str(val), ha="center",
        )
    ax.set_title(label)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### Pay by program year

The majority of respondents are in their first year. Neither overall pay nor
living wage ratio increases with seniority.

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Respondents, pay, and LW Ratio by program year (pay > $15 K)"

df_high = df[df["Overall_Pay"] > 15000].copy()
df_high["log_Overall_Pay"] = np.log10(df_high["Overall_Pay"])

year_order = [str(i) for i in range(1, 7)]
year_colors = ["#e6194b", "#3cb44b", "#ffe119", "#4363d8", "#f58231", "#911eb4"]
year_palette = dict(zip(year_order, year_colors))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(16, 5))

counts = df_high["Program_Year"].value_counts().reindex(year_order, fill_value=0)
axes[0].bar(
    counts.index, counts.values,
    color=[year_palette.get(y, "#999") for y in counts.index],
)
axes[0].set_title("Respondents per program year")

sns.boxplot(x="Program_Year", y="log_Overall_Pay", hue="Program_Year",
            data=df_high, order=year_order,
            palette=year_palette, legend=False, ax=axes[1])
axes[1].set_title("log10(Overall Pay) by program year")

sns.boxplot(x="Program_Year", y="LW_Ratio", hue="Program_Year",
            data=df_high, order=year_order,
            palette=year_palette, legend=False, ax=axes[2])
axes[2].set_title("LW Ratio by program year")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### Pay and Living Wage Ratio over time

Non-US universities were included from 2013 onwards (dotted line). Mean overall
pay has risen over time; living wage ratios declined internationally after 2014
before recovering after 2015.

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Mean Overall Pay and LW Ratio over time by location"

df_time = df[df["Overall_Pay"] > 15000].dropna(subset=["Academic_Year"])

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(10, 8))

for loc, color in [("us", COLOR_US), ("non_us", COLOR_NON_US)]:
    g = df_time[df_time["location"] == loc].groupby("Academic_Year")
    axes[0].plot(g["Overall_Pay"].mean().index, g["Overall_Pay"].mean().values,
                 label=loc, color=color, linewidth=2)
    axes[1].plot(g["LW_Ratio"].mean().index, g["LW_Ratio"].mean().values,
                 label=loc, color=color, linewidth=2)

for ax in axes:
    ax.axvline(pd.Timestamp("2013-01-01"), linestyle="dotted",
               color="black", linewidth=0.8)
    ax.axvline(pd.Timestamp("2015-01-01"), linestyle="dotted",
               color="black", linewidth=0.8)
    ax.legend()

axes[0].set_title("Mean Overall Pay over time")
axes[0].yaxis.set_major_formatter(
    mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f"${x:,.0f}")
)
axes[1].set_title("Mean LW Ratio over time")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### Pay and Living Wage Ratio by department

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Mean pay and LW Ratio by department for US and non-US universities"

df_dept = df[
    df["department_cleaned"].isin(top_dept) & (df["Overall_Pay"] > 15000)
]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(16, 12))

for row, (loc, color, title_suffix) in enumerate([
    ("us",     COLOR_US,     "US universities"),
    ("non_us", COLOR_NON_US, "non-US universities"),
]):
    subset = df_dept[df_dept["location"] == loc]

    pay_by_dept = subset.groupby("department_cleaned")["Overall_Pay"].mean().sort_values()
    lw_by_dept  = subset.groupby("department_cleaned")["LW_Ratio"].mean().sort_values()

    axes[row, 0].barh(pay_by_dept.index, pay_by_dept.values, color=color)
    axes[row, 0].set_title(f"Mean Overall Pay — {title_suffix}")
    axes[row, 0].xaxis.set_major_formatter(
        mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f"${x:,.0f}")
    )

    axes[row, 1].barh(lw_by_dept.index, lw_by_dept.values, color=color)
    axes[row, 1].set_title(f"Mean LW Ratio — {title_suffix}")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## Conclusions

- Overall Pay and Living Wage Ratio are higher in US universities.
- PhD student living wage ratios have decreased internationally since 2013, with recovery after 2015.
- Business students are the best paid in the USA; English students the least.
- Healthcare students receive the highest pay in non-US universities; communication students the least.

## References

1. [Original Data Source](http://www.phdstipends.com/results)
2. [LW Ratio definition](http://www.phdstipends.com/results)
3. [PhD salaries in the United States](https://academicpositions.be/career-advice/phd-professor-and-postdoc-salaries-in-the-united-states)
